name: Forest of Brocéliande
description: The Forest of Brocéliande, also known as Broceliande, is a legendary enchanted forest in Brittany, France, deeply intertwined with Arthurian legend and Celtic mythology. It is a place of mystery, magic, and perilous adventure, a realm where the boundaries between the mortal and the supernatural blur. Brocéliande is not merely a physical location but a symbolic space, representing the wild, untamed aspects of nature and the hidden realms of the spirit. Within its depths, knights errant encounter mythical creatures, powerful sorcerers, and tests of their courage and virtue. It is the home of the Lady of the Lake, Viviane, who raised Sir Lancelot and gifted Excalibur to Arthur. Merlin, the great enchanter, is also inextricably linked to Brocéliande, having been either imprisoned or choosing to remain within its enchanted confines. The forest's perpetual twilight, its whispering trees, and its hidden springs create an atmosphere of otherworldly beauty and danger, making it a central and evocative setting in Arthurian romances and folklore. The Vale of No Return, the Fountain of Barenton, and Merlin's Tomb are among its most famous landmarks, each imbued with its own unique magic and significance. Brocéliande embodies the mystical heart of Arthurian Britain, a place where destiny is forged and the veil between worlds is thin.
subtitle: Enchanted Forest of Arthurian Legend

keyMyths.title: Merlin's Imprisonment
keyMyths.description: Merlin is either imprisoned by Viviane within the Forest of Brocéliande or chooses to remain there, enchanted by its magic. This event marks the end of Merlin's active involvement in Arthurian affairs, but his presence lingers within the forest.

keyMyths.title: The Fountain of Barenton
keyMyths.description: The Fountain of Barenton is a magical spring within Brocéliande, said to possess the power to summon storms and reveal hidden truths. Knights often sought out the fountain to test their courage or seek guidance.

sources.author: Chrétien de Troyes
sources.work: Lancelot, the Knight of the Cart
sources.description: An early Arthurian romance featuring elements of Brocéliande.
sources.text: An early Arthurian romance featuring elements of Brocéliande.

sources.author: Sir Thomas Malory
sources.description: A comprehensive compilation of Arthurian legends, including references to Brocéliande.
